frontmatter.title: A Navigational Guide to Statistical Methods
frontmatter.subtitle: LS100 — Module 02 · Data Science & Statistics

content.name: Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial 4.0 International

children.value: A Navigational Guide to Statistical Methods
Project Leader & Instructor, Computational Behavioral Sciences, LS100, FAS, Harvard University

Behavioral data are often messy, count-heavy, zero-inflated, or tightly clustered within individuals, and rarely arrive in a clean, normally distributed package. Choosing the correct statistical tool requires diagnosing both the nature of your research question and the structural features of your data. This guide is a diagnostic map: instead of detailing the underlying mathematics, it tells you — given (a) your research question and (b) your data’s features — (c) which statistical test family is appropriate. Within each objective, tests are ordered from the most general/basic to the most case-specific/advanced tests. Use it together with the one-page decision-tree poster that accompanies this guide.

children.alt: Test-selection decision tree — the one-page map that accompanies this guide

children.value: Step 0: Characterize Your Question and Your Data

children.label: Step 0: Characterize Your Question and Your Data

children.value: Before opening any test, answer the following five questions. Your answers point directly to a section below (and to a branch of the decision-tree poster).

compare a sample to a baseline (§1), compare groups (§2–§3), measure an association (§4), build a predictive model (§5), handle nested/longitudinal/time data (§6), analyze angles/directions (§7), or quantify rater agreement (§8).
What type is the/ outcome (dependent) variable?
continuous, ordinal, nominal/binary, a count, a proportion, a time-to-event, or a circular angle.
What is the predictor / grouping structure?
none (one sample), one grouping with two vs. three-or-more groups, one or more continuous predictors, or several factors at once.
independent groups or paired/repeated/nested (the same individuals measured more than once).
Is the outcome roughly normal, or skewed/ordinal?
this decides parametric vs. non-parametric — confirm it with the diagnostics in §9.
for a quick glance of the appropriate statistical test for a given research question and features of the data.
A fast lookup for the most common situations. Find your row, then read the detailed entry in the section indicated.
Compare one mean to a fixed value
One-sample t test, Wilcoxon if skewed (§1)
Compare one proportion to a value
Welch’s / Student’s t-test, Mann-Whitney if skewed (§2)
Paired t-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank if skewed (§2)
One-way ANOVA + post-hoc test, Kruskal-Wallis if skewed (§3)
Repeated-measures ANOVA test, Friedman if skewed (§3)
Chi-square test, Fisher’s exact if small n (§4)
Poisson test, negative binomial if over-dispersed (§5)
Single-Sample Diagnostics: Comparing a Group to a Baseline

children.label: Single-Sample Diagnostics: Comparing a Group to a Baseline

children.value: Use these when you have a single sample and want to know if it differs from a known baseline, a theoretical constant, or a chance probability.
One continuous variable; data should look roughly bell-shaped (normally distributed).
To check if your sample’s mean differs from a known or hypothesized population mean.
Testing if the average response time of users on a new interface differs from the established industry baseline of 2.5 seconds.
Wilcoxon signed-rank test for a single sample
One continuous or highly ordinal variable; heavily skewed or non-normal.
The non-parametric alternative to the one-sample t-test, for data that violate normality or are ranked/ordinal.
Testing if the median satisfaction score (1–7 Likert) after an intervention differs from a neutral score of 4.
One binary categorical variable (success/failure); valid at any sample size.
To compare an observed proportion of a binary outcome against a hypothesized probability — the most basic single-sample categorical test.
Testing whether an animal chooses the left arm of a T-maze more often than the 50% expected by chance across 20 trials.
One-way chi-square test, multinomial goodness-of-fit test
One categorical variable (nominal or ordinal) with three or more categories.
To determine if an observed frequency distribution across categories matches an expected/theoretical distribution.
Investigating whether participants select three digital avatars with equal probability or prefer a specific one.
These assess differences between exactly two groups, conditions, or time points.
Student’s t-test, two-sample t-test, unpaired t-test
One categorical IV (2 mutually exclusive groups); one continuous DV. Assumes normality and equal variances.
The classic test to compare the average outcomes of two separate, independent groups.
Comparing average test scores of an experimental learning group against a traditional control group.
One categorical IV (2 groups); one continuous DV. Does NOT assume equal variances or equal group sizes.
The safer default for comparing two independent means — generally recommended over Student’s t whenever variances or group sizes may differ.
Comparing mean stride length between a small expert group and a larger novice group whose variability differs.
One categorical IV (2 groups); one ordinal or non-normal continuous DV.
The non-parametric equivalent of the independent-samples t-test, for skewed or ordinal outcomes.
Comparing daily social-media posts between two groups, where counts are highly skewed (many users post 0).
One categorical IV (2 groups); one binary DV (e.g., Yes/No). Requires a large sample (np ≥ 10 and n(1−p) ≥ 10).
Comparing the proportion of a specific outcome between two separate groups.
An A/B test comparing click-through rate between a blue and a green landing-page button.
Dependent t-test, repeated-measures t-test, matched-pairs t-test
Two continuous measurements from the same individuals (or matched pairs). Assumes the paired differences are normal.
To evaluate the difference between two related means, as in pre-test/post-test designs.
Measuring participant anxiety before and immediately after a stress-reduction session.
Two related measurements; uses only the direction (not the magnitude) of each pair’s difference. Minimal assumptions.
The simplest paired test, for when you can only trust the sign of each change (ordinal or grossly non-normal differences).
Whether posture symmetry improved (better/worse) after a training session, judged per participant.
Two related/paired measurements; ordinal or heavily skewed, non-normal differences.
The non-parametric equivalent of the paired t-test, for pre/post or matched designs violating normality.
Comparing self-reported urge to smoke (skewed 1–10 scale) before and after viewing a health-warning graphic.
3. Comparing Three or More Groups: Factorial & Multi-Variable Designs

children.label: 3. Comparing Three or More Groups: Factorial & Multi-Variable Designs

children.value: When your design expands beyond two groups or includes multiple intersecting factors.
One-factor analysis of variance, single-factor ANOVA
One categorical IV (≥ 3 groups); one continuous DV. Assumes normal residuals and equal variances.
To determine whether the means of three or more independent groups differ.
Comparing attention-span scores across silent rooms, rooms with classical music, and rooms with open-office noise.
One categorical IV (≥ 3 groups); one continuous DV with unequal variances.
A one-way comparison of 3+ means when variances differ across groups (the ANOVA analogue of Welch’s t).
Comparing mean call duration across three animal colonies whose variability differs markedly.
Kruskal-Wallis one-way ANOVA by ranks, non-parametric one-way ANOVA
One categorical IV (≥ 3 groups); one ordinal or non-normal continuous DV.
The non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA, for skewed or ordinal outcomes.
Comparing the ranking of product frustrations across three user-experience cohorts.
Tukey HSD, Games-Howell, Dunn’s test, Bonferroni/Holm correction
Follows a significant omnibus test (ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis).

After a significant omnibus result, to find WHICH specific group pairs differ while controlling the family-wise error rate — Tukey HSD (equal variances), Games-Howell (unequal variances), Dunn’s test (after Kruskal-Wallis).
An ANOVA shows attention differs across three sound environments; Tukey HSD identifies which pairs actually differ.
Multi-way ANOVA, factorial analysis of variance
Two or more categorical IVs (factors); one continuous DV.
To examine the simultaneous effect of two factors and, critically, their interaction (whether one factor’s effect depends on the other).
Testing how reward type (cash vs. praise) and task difficulty (easy vs. hard) interact to affect performance time.
One or more categorical IVs; one continuous DV; one or more continuous covariates to control for.
To compare group means while statistically controlling for a continuous confounding variable.
Comparing post-test comprehension across three teaching methods while controlling for baseline pre-test scores.
One or more categorical IVs; two or more related, correlated continuous DVs.
To test whether groups differ on a combined multivariate profile, rather than running separate ANOVAs per outcome.
Testing whether three personality types differ simultaneously across eye-fixation duration, click rate, and scrolling speed.
The same subjects measured across ≥ 3 conditions or time points; one continuous DV. Assumes sphericity.
When tracking change across multiple time points, or exposing the same participants to 3+ treatments.
Tracking cognitive fatigue measured at hour 1, hour 3, and hour 5 of a continuous testing block.
Friedman two-way ANOVA by ranks, non-parametric repeated-measures ANOVA
One within-subjects factor (≥ 3 related conditions/time points); ordinal or non-normal continuous DV.
The non-parametric alternative to repeated-measures ANOVA; pair with a Dunn’s or Nemenyi post-hoc.
Comparing a skewed comfort rating from the same users across three prosthetic settings.
One within-subjects factor (≥ 3 related conditions); one binary DV.
The extension of McNemar’s test to three or more repeated binary measurements.
Whether the same animals succeed/fail at a task across three successive training days.
Split-plot ANOVA, between-within ANOVA, mixed factorial ANOVA
At least one between-subjects factor (different groups) and at least one within-subjects factor (repeated measures); one continuous DV.
The classic randomized-controlled-trial framework: does a treatment group change over time differently than a control group?
Measuring depression scores at pre-test, post-test, and 6-month follow-up (within) for a treatment and a control group (between).
These assess how variables change together, whether continuous scales or categorical labels.
Two continuous variables. Assumes a linear relationship, normality, and homoscedasticity.
To measure the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two continuous metrics.
Relating total hours spent on an educational platform to final exam grade.
Two continuous or ordinal variables. No normality/linearity assumption; tracks monotonic relationships.
The non-parametric alternative to Pearson, for curved relationships or ranked data.
Correlating a user’s self-reported stress rank with their rank of daily screen time.
Kendall’s τ coefficient, Kendall’s rank correlation
A non-parametric alternative optimal for small samples or many tied ranks; more robust than Spearman in sparse data.
Correlating preference rankings of 8 interface mockups between two small focus groups.
A special case of the Pearson correlation
One continuous variable and one binary categorical variable.
To quantify the association between a continuous measure and a two-level grouping (equivalent to an independent t-test, expressed as a correlation).
Relating movement smoothness to group membership (trained vs. untrained).
Two continuous variables plus one or more continuous control variables.
To measure the association between two variables while holding a third constant, removing its confounding influence.
Relating practice hours to performance while controlling for baseline fitness.
Pearson’s chi-square test, chi-square bivariate test
Two categorical variables. Large samples where every expected cell count is ≥ 5.
To determine whether two categorical variables are significantly associated.
Testing whether political affiliation is associated with preferred media medium (text, video, audio).
Two categorical variables, typically a 2 × 2 table. Used when samples are small or events rare (any expected cell < 5).
An exact calculation used when the large-sample approximation of the chi-square test breaks down.
Whether a rare side effect is associated with a new drug vs. placebo in a small study.
Two paired/dependent nominal variables, structured as a 2 × 2 table of before/after binary states.
To analyze changes in paired categorical proportions — also the standard test for comparing two classifiers on the same test set.
Whether an ad campaign shifts voter preference (support/not) among the same individuals polled before and after.
5. Explanatory Modeling & Prediction: The Regression Family

children.label: 5. Explanatory Modeling & Prediction: The Regression Family

children.value: When the goal shifts from testing differences to building predictive equations or isolating the contribution of multiple predictors. Most of these belong to the Generalized Linear Model (GLM) family, which differ mainly in the assumed distribution of the outcome.
One continuous predictor; one continuous outcome. Assumes linearity, independent and normal residuals, homoscedasticity.
To predict a continuous outcome from a single predictor.
Predicting annual charitable donation from a self-reported empathy score.
Multiple continuous or dummy-coded predictors; one continuous outcome.
To isolate the impact of several factors on a continuous outcome simultaneously, controlling for confounds.
Modeling productivity from sleep, coffee, experience, and remote-work status.
Multiple mixed predictors; one binary outcome (0/1).
Predicting the probability of a binary event or choice.
Modeling whether a shopper completes a purchase or abandons the cart from browsing time and discount.
Multiple mixed predictors; one ordered categorical outcome (e.g., Low/Medium/High). Assumes proportional odds.
Predicting an inherently ranked but non-continuous outcome.
Predicting treatment compliance (non/partial/full) from demographic and psychological scales.
Multiple mixed predictors; one nominal outcome with ≥ 3 unordered choices.
Modeling discrete-choice behavior when options have no inherent ranking.
Predicting which of four app themes a user selects from their personality and age.
Multiple mixed predictors; a count outcome (non-negative integers). Assumes the mean equals the variance.
Modeling behavioral frequencies over a fixed window of time or space.
Modeling monthly support tickets from software-utilization metrics.
Multiple mixed predictors; a count outcome whose variance exceeds its mean (over-dispersion).
The standard correction when count data show heavy tails and extreme variation, violating Poisson assumptions.
Modeling daily phone checks, where a few super-users inflate the variance.
Count outcomes with far more zeros than a standard distribution predicts.
When zeros arise from two processes: ‘structural zeros’ (cannot/never perform the behavior) and ‘true zeros’ (could, but scored zero this time).
Modeling daily cigarettes smoked: non-smokers always log 0, while quitting smokers may happen to log 0 on a given day.
A continuous outcome strictly bounded between 0 and 1 (rates, proportions, fractions, excluding exactly 0 and 1).
Predicting percentages or bounded allocations that show heteroscedasticity near the boundaries.
Predicting the proportion of a monthly budget allocated to entertainment from psychometric scores.
6. Advanced: Clustered, Longitudinal, Survival & Time-Series Frameworks

children.label: 6. Advanced: Clustered, Longitudinal, Survival & Time-Series Frameworks

children.value: For data that violate the assumption of independent observations, track hidden constructs, or unfold over time.
Hierarchical linear modeling (HLM), multilevel modeling (MLM), random-effects models
Data that are nested or clustered (repeated trials within participants; students within schools).
Essential for behavioral data with dependencies. Splits effects into fixed effects (variables of interest) and random effects (baseline variation across individuals/clusters).
Modeling how a learning game affects performance across 30 trials, allowing each player their own baseline skill.
A time-to-event outcome with right-censoring; one categorical grouping.
To estimate ‘time-until-behavior’ curves and compare them between groups — the descriptive/comparative step before Cox modeling.
Comparing how long until animals in two enrichment conditions first use a novel object.
Predictors mapped to a time-to-event outcome with right-censoring.
Modeling how predictors affect the time until a behavior or milestone occurs.
Modeling days until a user cancels a subscription from engagement telemetry.
To quantify how strongly two signals co-vary at different time lags — used to detect synchrony and lead-lag relationships between movement or physiological signals.
Measuring whether one dancer’s motion leads or follows a partner’s, from the lag of peak correlation.
Elastic sequence matching (a similarity measure, not a hypothesis test)
Two sequences (e.g., pose or gesture time series) that may differ in speed or timing.
To measure similarity between two movement sequences while allowing local stretching/compression in time — foundational for gesture matching and behavior clustering.
Quantifying how closely a learner’s tennis-serve trajectory matches an expert template despite a different tempo.
Box-Jenkins models; VAR for multiple parallel series
High-frequency sequential observations from one entity, where values correlate with their own past (autocorrelation).
Analyzing sensor streams or behavioral trends over continuous timelines to forecast future behavior.
Forecasting next month’s daily active users from three years of history, filtering weekly and seasonal cycles.
A structural-equation framework on longitudinal data with repeated measurements over explicit time frames.
To model individual trajectories of growth, decline, or learning over time — not just group averages.
Tracking how a child’s socio-emotional development trajectory shifts year-over-year from ages 5 to 12.
Latent Structure Discovery (PCA → EFA → CFA → SEM)

children.label: Latent Structure Discovery (PCA → EFA → CFA → SEM)

children.value: Many correlated variables believed to reflect a smaller set of hidden constructs.

A progression from data reduction to theory testing: PCA (compress correlated variables into uncorrelated components), EFA (discover hidden constructs), CFA (verify a predefined measurement structure), SEM (test complex multi-path theories with direct, indirect, and mediated effects).
Reducing a battery of behavioral items to a few factors, then testing a theorized pathway among them with SEM.
k-means, hierarchical clustering, silhouette score, PCA / UMAP / t-SNE (exploratory, not hypothesis tests)
Many continuous features per observation, with no outcome labels.

To DISCOVER groupings or compress structure rather than test a hypothesis — clustering groups similar observations, the silhouette score judges cluster quality, and PCA/UMAP/t-SNE reduce dimensions for visualization. Confirm any discovered groups with independent hypothesis tests.
Clustering extracted audio embeddings to reveal vocalization types, or grouping postures from pose features.

Angles and directions — joint angles, movement heading, time of day — live on a circle, where 0° and 360° are identical. Ordinary linear statistics mishandle this wrap-around (the mean of 350° and 10° is 0°, not 180°), so circular-specific methods are required. These are directly relevant to pose and movement analysis.
To test whether angles are uniformly spread around the circle versus clustered around a single mean direction.
Testing whether an animal’s movement headings cluster in a preferred direction rather than being random.
One set of angles plus a hypothesized direction.
Like the Rayleigh test, but more powerful when you have an a-priori expected direction.
Testing whether escape headings concentrate toward a known shelter direction.
Angles from two or more groups, reasonably concentrated (von Mises distributed).
To compare the MEAN directions of two or more groups — the circular analogue of ANOVA.
Comparing mean limb-swing angle at toe-off between sprinters and joggers.
To compare two circular distributions without assuming a von Mises shape.
Comparing the distribution of head-orientation angles between two behavioral states.
Two angular variables, or one angular and one linear variable.
To measure association involving angular data.
Relating joint-angle phase to a continuous speed measure during gait.

When several coders annotate behavior, or a measure is repeated, you must quantify how consistent those measurements are before trusting them — a routine step whenever a team builds an annotation schema.
To measure agreement between two coders beyond what chance alone would produce.
Two students independently labeling each video frame as groom / walk / rest.
Three or more raters; categorical labels.
The extension of Cohen’s kappa to more than two raters.
A team of four annotators coding courtship behaviors.
Any number of raters; nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio data; tolerates missing ratings.
The most general agreement coefficient — useful when coders skip items or data types are mixed.
Assessing agreement on an ordinal aggression-intensity scale where some clips were not coded by everyone.
Continuous measurements; two or more raters, or repeated measurements.
To quantify agreement/consistency for continuous ratings, and for test-retest reliability of a measure.
Agreement among three raters’ continuous limb-angle estimates, or the stability of an automated metric across repeated recordings.
Two continuous measurement methods applied to the same units.
To visualize and quantify agreement between two measurement methods (e.g., manual vs. automated) — distinct from their correlation.
Comparing joint angles from manual digitization against pose-estimation output.
9. Before You Test: Diagnostics, Robust & Resampling Methods

children.label: 9. Before You Test: Diagnostics, Robust & Resampling Methods

children.value: Choosing between parametric and non-parametric tests — and trusting any result — depends on first checking assumptions. These tools are how you decide.
Shapiro-Wilk test, Kolmogorov-Smirnov / Anderson-Darling, Q-Q plot
One continuous variable, or a model’s residuals.
To judge whether the normality assumption holds. A small p-value (or a curved Q-Q plot) signals non-normality — consider a non-parametric or robust alternative.
Checking whether stride-length residuals are normal before performing an ANOVA.
A continuous variable across two or more groups.
To test whether groups share equal variances (an assumption of Student’s t and standard ANOVA). If violated, switch to Welch’s t / Welch’s ANOVA.
Confirming equal variance of reaction times across conditions before a standard ANOVA.
To obtain a p-value by repeatedly shuffling labels rather than assuming a theoretical distribution — ideal for small or oddly-shaped behavioral samples.
Testing a group difference in a small movement study by permuting condition labels 10,000 times.
To estimate confidence intervals and standard errors for any metric (means, medians, correlations, custom indices) without distributional assumptions.
A 95% confidence interval for a custom gait-symmetry index by resampling participants.
(Cohen’s d, η², r, odds ratio) alongside every p-value, and
(Bonferroni, Holm, or FDR) whenever you run many tests.
